RULING: Argued and the Court ruled as follows: DEFENDANT 1207 INDIANA STREET ASSOCIATES LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION is Granted as to the tenth cause of action for declaratory relief regarding the duty to defend and denied as to all other causes of action. For the tenth cause of action, under Contract, 3.18 and Civil Code 2778(4), Phelan Construction had a duty to defend once 1207 Indiana Street Associates, LLC properly tendered its defense. (Weiner Decl., Ex. B.) Moving party met it's burden on this issue. The burden then shifted to Phelan Construction. Phelan Construction has not demonstrated a triable issue of fact that tender was not proper. The "duty [to defend] arises immediately upon a proper tender of defense by the indemnitee, and thus before the litigation to be defended has determined whether indemnity is actually owed. This duty, as described in the statute [Civil Code 2778(4)], therefore cannot depend on the outcome of that litigation. It follows that, under subdivision 4 of section 2778, claims 'embraced by the indemnity,' as to which the duty to defend is owed, include those which, at the time of tender, allege facts that would give rise to a duty of indemnity." (Crawford v. Weather Shield Mfg. (2008) 44 Cal.4th 541, 547.) Further, Phelan has not come forward with any evidence of a triable issue of fack as to whether the duty to defend is applicable here. Accordingly, Phelan has an obligation under its contract to provide a defense to cross-complainant in this action. To the extent the motion for summary adjudication requests an award of attorney fees, that is denied. First, it is not noticed in the motion. Second, to the extent moving party is seeking reimbursement for attorney fees prior to tender, those fees, if recoverable, are not incident to the duty to defend under Section 2778(4). See Watsonville v. Corrigan (2007) 149 Cal. App. 4th 1542, 1549. Further, there is a triable issue of fact as to the fees. Summary adjudication is denied as to the third (express indemnity) and eleventh cause of action for declaratory relief regarding the duty of indemnity. Cross-Complainant has not met its initial burden. There has been no recovery against Cross-Complainant in the underlying Complaint. See Crawford, supra, 44 Cal. 4th at 559 ("one can only indemnify against 'claims for damages' that have been resolved against the indemnitee, i.e., those as to which the indemnitee has actually sustained liability or paid damages") Summary adjudication is denied on the twelfth causes of action, since Cross-Complainant's motion would not dispose of the entire cause of action, and no exception applies. (See CCP 437c(s).)(Prevailing party to prepare a form of order.) Judge: Marla Miller, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Reporter: Anthony Vaughn #6185.
